meta.title: Hudson, Ohio · Peer Benchmark
meta.subtitle: Services and amenities across 14 Ohio cities

hero_image.credit: Hudson Clock Tower, Historic District. Photo by Kevin Payravi via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0.

cities.name: Hudson
cities.signature_amenity: Endowed library + municipal fiber + clock-tower downtown
cities.distinctive: Western Reserve village with a privately-endowed library, municipal electric, and municipal fiber.

photo.credit: Photo by Kevin Payravi via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

utilities.water: City of Hudson water (Aqua Ohio source)
utilities.ev_chargers: Yes — at City Hall and select parks

civic.library: Hudson Library & Historical Society — privately endowed; National Medal for Museum and Library Service (2009)
civic.community_center: No standalone city rec center; library serves much of that role
civic.senior_center: Yes — programs through Hudson Community Service

amenities.parks_pools: Hudson Springs Park, Cascade Park, multiple township parks; splash pad at the Reserve at Hudson Springs
amenities.trails: Hudson Springs loop, Veterans Way Trail; regional connections to Towpath / Summit Metro Parks
amenities.pickleball: Courts at Cascade Park; growing program
amenities.library: Hudson Library & Historical Society — privately endowed, 2009 National Medal winner; flagship cultural institution
amenities.community_center: No standalone city rec center; programs run through Parks & Rec and partner orgs
amenities.ice_rink: Regional (Kent State / Bay Village indirectly via OAHL)
amenities.public_art: Outdoor public sculpture, historic plaques along the Green
amenities.farmers_market: Hudson Farmers' Market — Saturdays in season, on the Green
amenities.concert_series: Music on the Green summer series
amenities.holiday_lighting: Iconic First & Main + Green lighting display — the signature Hudson tradition
amenities.deer_management: Controlled deer-hunting / archery program on city land
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA; active tree commission and arborist
amenities.ev_chargers: Public chargers at City Hall and select parks
amenities.downtown: First & Main mixed-use district + historic Green — the canonical Hudson postcard

cities.downtown: First & Main + the Green — a textbook walkable Western Reserve downtown

schools.note: Routinely top-5 in Ohio Dept of Ed rankings; major driver of home values

cities.name: Solon
cities.signature_amenity: Solon Community Center — pools, climbing wall, current stream
cities.distinctive: Tightest size match to Hudson; world-class community-center facility; major commercial base.

photo.credit: Photo by Mayor Pez (Wikipedia user) via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

utilities.sewer: City of Solon municipal sanitary sewer

civic.library: Solon Branch · Cuyahoga County Public Library
civic.rec_center_features: Indoor + outdoor pool complex with lazy river, zero-entry, waterslides; rock climbing wall; gym; jogging track; aerobics studio; free-weight room; hot tub
civic.senior_center: Solon Senior Center (attached to Community Center; free for residents)

amenities.parks_pools: Solon Community Center indoor+outdoor pool complex with lazy river and waterslides plus Community Park.
amenities.trails: Walking paths through park system and along the Community Center campus.
amenities.pickleball: Available through Recreation programming; specific court count not published.
amenities.dog_parks: Not prominently published on city site.
amenities.library: Solon Branch of Cuyahoga County Public Library — a top branch in a top-rated CCPL system.
amenities.community_center: Solon Community Center is a true full-service rec center: climbing wall, indoor + outdoor pool, gym, track.
amenities.senior_center: Dedicated Solon Senior Center attached to the Community Center; free for residents.
amenities.public_art: Solon Center for the Arts (city department) offers gallery exhibits, classes, theater & music.
amenities.farmers_market: Solon Farmers Market — Wednesdays 4-7pm at Bicentennial Park during the season.
amenities.concert_series: Summer concert programming through Solon Center for the Arts/Parks & Rec.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Annual community events on city calendar.
amenities.deer_management: Formal Solon Deer Management Plan published on city site.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA status not specifically confirmed on city site.
amenities.downtown: No traditional walkable downtown; commercial spine along SOM Center and Aurora roads.

cities.name: Aurora
cities.signature_amenity: Geauga Lake-era nostalgia + Sun Run trails
cities.distinctive: Hudson's closest NE Ohio twin on demographics; quieter civic profile.

photo.alt: Church and town hall, Aurora Center Historic District
photo.credit: Photo by Nyttend via Wikimedia Commons, Public Domain

utilities.stormwater_fee: Storm drain charge bundled in utility rate

civic.library: Aurora Memorial Library · Portage County District Library
civic.rec_center_features: 24/7 fitness center, group fitness, gym, activity rooms, leagues; separate seasonal Aurora Aquatic Center
civic.senior_center: Senior programming via Parks & Rec; no stand-alone facility

amenities.parks_pools: Aurora Aquatic Center (seasonal outdoor); 1,800+ acres of park/greenspace across 13+ named parks.
amenities.trails: Sunny Lake's 1.75-mile paved loop plus multiple branching hiking trails through park system.
amenities.dog_parks: Dedicated dog park within park system.
amenities.library: Aurora Memorial Library is the Aurora branch of Portage County District Library.
amenities.community_center: Aurora Recreation Community Center (ARCC) — fitness center, leagues, gym, activity rooms.
amenities.senior_center: Senior programming via Parks & Rec rather than a dedicated facility.
amenities.concert_series: Summer programming via Parks & Rec community events.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Tree lighting and seasonal events on city calendar.
amenities.deer_management: Annual deer hunting permit lottery administered by Aurora Police.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA since 1992; Street Tree Program nationally recognized by National Arbor Day Foundation.
amenities.downtown: No traditional walkable downtown; commercial nodes along Rt 43/Chillicothe.

cities.name: Bay Village
cities.signature_amenity: Cahoon Memorial Park + Lake Erie lakefront
cities.distinctive: West-side lakefront old-money suburb; smaller, denser than Hudson.

photo.alt: Huntington Beach, Lake Erie, with Cleveland skyline
photo.credit: Photo by Heather McLaughlin via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 2.0

civic.library: Bay Village Branch · CCPL (rebuilt 2022, 17,000 sf, rooftop terrace overlooking Lake Erie, Innovation Center)
civic.rec_center_name: Campus model — Community House (1882) + Community Gym + Aquatic Center + school gyms

amenities.parks_pools: Seasonal outdoor Aquatic Center; multiple lakeside parks; no indoor pool.
amenities.trails: West Interurban Connector Trail and Interurban Pedestrian Bridge connect to Lake Erie.
amenities.pickleball: Adult pickleball programming through Rec Department.
amenities.dog_parks: Not listed on city Parks & Rec site.
amenities.library: Brand-new (2022) CCPL Bay Village Branch with Lake Erie rooftop terrace — architecturally distinctive.
amenities.community_center: Campus model: 1882 Community House + Community Gym + Aquatic Center + school gyms.
amenities.public_art: BAYarts cultural campus (independent nonprofit) hosts gallery, classes, and farmers market.
amenities.farmers_market: BAYarts Farmers Market (seasonal, at BAYarts campus).
amenities.concert_series: Summer programming through Rec and BAYarts.
amenities.deer_management: Formal deer management program on city site.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA status not confirmed on city site.
amenities.downtown: Small Dover Center / Wolf Road commercial node; no traditional walkable downtown.

cities.name: Rocky River
cities.signature_amenity: Summer Concert Series + Rocky River Reservation access
cities.distinctive: West-side Cuyahoga peer on culture and geography; median income lower than other Tier A.

photo.alt: Aerial view of Rocky River harbor and Lake Erie entrance
photo.credit: Photo by Ken Winters, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers via Wikimedia Commons, Public Domain

utilities.sewer: Rocky River Division of Sewer Billing (city-managed)

civic.library: Rocky River Public Library — INDEPENDENT municipal library
civic.rec_center_features: Indoor pool, seasonal outdoor pool, gym/fitness, classes; campus shared with Hamilton Ice Arena
civic.senior_center: Rocky River Senior Center (free to join; M-F 8:30-4:30)

amenities.parks_pools: Indoor pool + seasonal outdoor municipal pool; 10 named parks including lakefront Rocky River Park and Bradstreet's Landing.
amenities.trails: Adjacent to Cleveland Metroparks Rocky River Reservation; sledding hills at Morley and Rocky River parks.
amenities.pickleball: 12 pickleball courts at Tri City Park plus courts at Linden Park.
amenities.dog_parks: Not listed in city parks page.
amenities.library: Independent Rocky River Public Library — its own taxing district, not part of CCPL.
amenities.community_center: Full Recreation Center on municipal campus including indoor pool.
amenities.senior_center: Dedicated Rocky River Senior Center — free to join.
amenities.ice_rink: Hamilton Ice Arena (city-owned, on City Hall Park campus) — distinctive among peers.
amenities.farmers_market: Not prominently published on city site.
amenities.concert_series: Summer Concert Series via Rec Department.
amenities.downtown: Beachcliff / Old Detroit commercial district — boutique shops, cafes, dining.

cities.name: Powell
cities.distinctive: Higher median income than Hudson; newer-built growth suburb; Olentangy schools.

amenities.parks_pools: 10 parks (~125 ac) including Village Green and Adventure Park; no municipal pool (relies on private/HOA).
amenities.trails: Nearly 30 miles of paved bike/multi-use trails connecting parks and neighborhoods.
amenities.pickleball: Courts at city parks; no dedicated complex.
amenities.dog_parks: Not a flagship; off-leash via county/township options.
amenities.library: Served by Delaware County District Library (independent county system).
amenities.senior_center: Programs only; no standalone senior facility.
amenities.farmers_market: Powell Chamber Farmers Market — Saturdays May-October at 240 N. Liberty St.
amenities.concert_series: Community Band Concert Series at Village Green in summer.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Holidays in Powell tree-lighting and seasonal events.
amenities.deer_management: No formal city-led culling program identified.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA; forestry via Public Works.
amenities.ev_chargers: Limited public EV; no documented municipal program.
amenities.downtown: Historic downtown around four corners (Olentangy/Liberty); Village Green civic core.

cities.name: Bexley
cities.signature_amenity: Jeffrey Mansion / Jeffrey Park + Capital University proximity
cities.distinctive: Old, walkable, dense Columbus inner suburb; backdoor trash pickup; landlocked.

photo.credit: Photo by Sixflashphoto via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

utilities.sewer: City of Bexley local; Columbus treatment

civic.rec_center_name: Jeffrey Mansion (Parks & Rec HQ, not a fitness center)

amenities.parks_pools: Jeffrey Park (34 ac, gifted 1941) anchors the system; David H. Madison Community Pool is the seasonal outdoor pool.
amenities.trails: Boardwalk along Alum Creek in Jeffrey Park; limited mileage due to dense first-ring grid.
amenities.pickleball: Court availability at parks; no dedicated complex.
amenities.dog_parks: No major dedicated dog park within city limits.
amenities.library: Bexley Public Library — fully independent single-branch system (separate from CML).
amenities.community_center: No purpose-built rec/community center; Jeffrey Mansion houses programming and admin.
amenities.senior_center: Dedicated Bexley Senior Center with programming.
amenities.public_art: Ad hoc + Capital University partnerships (Mees Hall, Schumacher Gallery); no formal master plan.
amenities.farmers_market: Bexley Farmers Market (seasonal, Cassady Ave / Capital University).
amenities.concert_series: Jazz in the Park summer series at Jeffrey Park.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Annual Tree Lighting + Light Collection winter display (Dec 1-22).
amenities.deer_management: No formal program identified on city site.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA; Earth & Arbor Month programming.
amenities.downtown: Main Street / Bexley Square along East Main — walkable district with shops and restaurants.

schools.note: Small, high-performing district with strong arts/academics

cities.name: Worthington
cities.signature_amenity: Year-round Farmers Market + Griswold Center for seniors
cities.distinctive: Historic Columbus inner suburb with rare year-round farmers market; dedicated senior facility.

photo.alt: Downtown Worthington, Old Worthington Historic District
photo.credit: Photo by Nyttend via Wikimedia Commons, Public Domain

utilities.ev_chargers: Public EV at City facilities + Community Center area

civic.library: Worthington Libraries — INDEPENDENT multi-branch (Old Worthington, Northwest, Worthington Park)
civic.rec_center_name: Worthington Community Center (345 E. Wilson Bridge Rd)

amenities.parks_pools: 221+ acres; Worthington Community Center includes aquatics; outdoor pool program; McCord Park recently renovated.
amenities.trails: Olentangy River Parklands connect to regional Olentangy Trail; bike/multi-use network.
amenities.pickleball: Courts at parks + Community Center programming.
amenities.library: Worthington Libraries — independent system, nationally recognized, three branches.
amenities.community_center: Worthington Community Center — full-service rec with fitness, gym, aquatics.
amenities.senior_center: Griswold Center — dedicated 55+ facility (fitness, crafts, library, social).
amenities.public_art: Public art via Worthington Arts Council partnerships; not a Dublin-scale master plan.
amenities.farmers_market: Worthington Farmers Market — YEAR-ROUND (summer outdoor in Old Worthington / winter indoor Nov-Apr at Shops at Worthington Place); est. 1987.
amenities.concert_series: Summer concert series in Old Worthington (Village Green).
amenities.holiday_lighting: Old Worthington holiday lighting + Market Day events.
amenities.deer_management: Formal Deer Management Program; targeted removal by USDA Wildlife Services Jan-Feb 2026.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA; active urban forestry.
amenities.ev_chargers: Public EV at City facilities and Community Center.
amenities.downtown: Old Worthington along High Street — colonial-era walkable historic district anchored by Village Green.

cities.name: New Albany
cities.signature_amenity: 16-court pickleball complex + 80 miles of leisure trails
cities.distinctive: Master-planned Georgian community; the highest-income city in this benchmark.

photo.credit: Photo by Sixflashphoto via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

civic.library: Columbus Metropolitan Library — New Albany Branch (200 Market St)
civic.rec_center_name: Philip Heit Center for Healthy New Albany (current) + Fieldhouse & Community Center (185,890 sf, opening 2026)
civic.senior_center: Programming via Healthy New Albany / Heit Center; no dedicated facility

amenities.parks_pools: Bevelhymer Park, Thompson Park, Wexner Park + extensive joint park district facilities; relies on private swim clubs (no municipal outdoor pool).
amenities.trails: Over 50 miles of paved leisure trails — the signature amenity, connecting neighborhoods, parks, and Village Center. ('80+' figure circulates but is NOT confirmed by official sources.)
amenities.pickleball: 16-court tournament-grade pickleball complex at 7300 Walnut St (opened 2023) — among the largest free public complexes in the Columbus region.
amenities.dog_parks: No flagship dog park identified in NA Parks system.
amenities.library: Columbus Metropolitan Library — New Albany Branch.
amenities.community_center: Philip Heit Center for Healthy New Albany (current) + 185,890 sf Fieldhouse & Community Center opening 2026 (indoor track, basketball, volleyball, pickleball).
amenities.senior_center: Programming via Healthy New Albany; no senior-only building.
amenities.public_art: Public art via Rose Run Park + New Albany Company partnerships; not Dublin-scale formal program.
amenities.farmers_market: Healthy New Albany Farmers Market — Thursdays July-August, Market Square.
amenities.concert_series: Summer Concert Series at Charleen & Charles Hinson Amphitheater.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Holiday lighting in Village Center / Market Square.
amenities.deer_management: No formal city-led culling program documented.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA; active community forestry.
amenities.ev_chargers: Public EV at municipal + corporate-campus locations; not centrally documented.
amenities.downtown: New Albany Village Center / Market Square — planned, Georgian-style walkable mixed-use core.

schools.note: Single unified K-12 campus; Amazon-sponsored 'Think Big' STEM corners

cities.name: Upper Arlington
cities.signature_amenity: Bob Crane / new community center + Tremont Library
cities.distinctive: Larger than peer band; the Ohio 'gold-standard' historic suburb. 'Old Arlington' core matches Hudson character.

photo.credit: Photo by Pi.1415926535 via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

utilities.ev_chargers: Public EV at Municipal Services Center + Bob Crane CC

civic.library: Upper Arlington Public Library — INDEPENDENT, multi-branch (est. 1967 via Ohio HB 494)
civic.rec_center_name: Bob Crane Community Center (3200 Tremont Rd)
civic.senior_center: Through Bob Crane CC / Parks & Rec

amenities.parks_pools: Three outdoor pools (Memorial Day-Labor Day) + Bob Crane indoor aquatics; flagship parks: Northam, Thompson, Fancyburg, Reed Road.
amenities.trails: Tremont Road and neighborhood bike paths; integrated with Columbus regional trail system.
amenities.pickleball: Courts at Northam and other parks + indoor at Bob Crane CC.
amenities.dog_parks: Dog park within Parks & Rec system.
amenities.library: Upper Arlington Public Library — independent, multi-branch, locally governed since 1967.
amenities.community_center: Bob Crane Community Center: ~165,000 sf, 5 floors, indoor pool, two gyms, walking track, art gallery, e-sports room, cafe. Top OPRA awards.
amenities.senior_center: Senior programs through Bob Crane CC; no separate facility.
amenities.public_art: Public art at Northam, Miller Park; not Dublin-scale master plan.
amenities.farmers_market: Upper Arlington Farmers Market — Wednesdays 3-6 pm, May-September.
amenities.concert_series: UA Performance Series + Sounds of Summer at Mallway / Tremont Center.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Annual tree-lighting + holiday events at Mallway Park.
amenities.deer_management: Discussion ongoing; no formal sharpshooter program (vs. Worthington's).
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA since 1990; annual Arbor Day planting.
amenities.ev_chargers: Public EV at Municipal Services Center + Bob Crane CC.
amenities.downtown: No traditional downtown; commercial nodes at Lane Ave, Kingsdale, Tremont/Arlington Ave.

schools.note: Single comprehensive HS district; nationally recognized academics

cities.name: Montgomery
cities.distinctive: Upscale Cincinnati 'village' with strong civic identity; better Cincinnati peer than Wyoming/Mariemont on size+wealth.

photo.credit: Photo by Nyttend via Wikimedia Commons, Public Domain

utilities.ev_chargers: Not municipally operated; not mentioned on city site

civic.library: Served by Blue Ash and Symmes Township branches of CHPL; no in-city branch
civic.senior_center: No dedicated senior center; programming via Parks & Rec

amenities.parks_pools: Eight parks + nature preserve (96+ ac): Swaim 12.7 ac, Pioneer 21.1 ac, Dulle, Montgomery, Weller. Municipal outdoor pool with wading pool, water slide, diving board.
amenities.trails: ~30+ mi of connecting sidewalks and paved loop trails through park system.
amenities.pickleball: Tennis/basketball courts at Swaim Park; no dedicated municipal pickleball.
amenities.library: Served by Blue Ash + Symmes Township CHPL branches; no in-city library.
amenities.community_center: No municipal community center; Terwilliger Lodge is a rental venue.
amenities.senior_center: No dedicated senior center; programs via Parks & Rec.
amenities.public_art: Beautification & Tree Commission; no formal public art program.
amenities.farmers_market: No dedicated municipal farmers market; downtown DORA + food tours instead.
amenities.concert_series: BAMSO Independence Day Concert at Montgomery Park; live music at Bastille Day.
amenities.deer_management: 2002 Council adopted non-lethal/education policy; controlled hunts deemed unsafe due to density. Limited bow hunting at Gate of Heaven Cemetery only.
amenities.tree_program: Beautification & Tree Commission active; Tree City USA status not confirmed on city site.
amenities.downtown: Historic core along Montgomery & Cooper Roads; DORA designated; signature Bastille Day festival anchors the district.

schools.note: Shared district with Blue Ash, Symmes Twp, parts of Sycamore Twp; Montgomery Elementary in city

cities.name: Chagrin Falls
cities.signature_amenity: Literal waterfall in the middle of downtown
cities.distinctive: Tiny village with high wealth and a postcard downtown; user-requested.

photo.alt: Chagrin Falls — the literal waterfall in downtown
photo.credit: Photo by AngelP8823 via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 4.0

utilities.water: Village of Chagrin Falls — operates its OWN water utility (100+ years)
utilities.sewer: Village of Chagrin Falls municipal sewer

civic.library: Chagrin Falls Branch · Cuyahoga County Public Library

amenities.parks_pools: No municipal pool; recreation centered on village parks and Chagrin River.
amenities.trails: Whitesburg Park walking trails along Chagrin River; Riverside Park.
amenities.pickleball: Pickleball court at River Run Park (rare for a village this size).
amenities.dog_parks: Dog park at River Run Park.
amenities.library: Chagrin Falls Branch of CCPL in the historic downtown.
amenities.community_center: None — relies on partner facilities and Village Hall meeting spaces.
amenities.public_art: Heavy arts presence via Valley Art Center and downtown galleries (independent nonprofits).
amenities.farmers_market: Chagrin Falls Farmers Market (seasonal, Sundays at Triangle Park; long-running).
amenities.concert_series: Summer concerts on the Triangle and at Riverside Park.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Major holiday lighting on Main Street; New Year's Eve Giant Popcorn Ball Drop (signature).
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA not directly confirmed.
amenities.downtown: One of the most walkable, intact historic downtowns in NE Ohio — anchored by the falls, the Popcorn Shop (1875), and Main Street. Blossom Time Festival since 1930s.

costs.tax_caveat: Re-verify via RITA — historical 1.5% may have increased

sources.label: Chagrin Falls Holidays / Popcorn Ball Drop

cities.name: Madeira
cities.distinctive: Small wealthy Cincinnati boutique suburb; school-driven.

photo.credit: Photo by Ed! (Wikipedia user) via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 3.0

utilities.water: Transitioning Nov 2025: dual GCWW/Indian Hill Water Works → GCWW-only over ~2 years (Ord. 25-12)

amenities.parks_pools: Three parks led by McDonald Commons (flagship, $13.5M 2023-24 renovation); Sellman Park (10 ac, 2 lighted tennis); Hosbrook Bird Sanctuary (2 ac). Pool model is PRIVATE — Madeira Swim & Tennis Club (residents only).
amenities.trails: Walking paths in parks; no dedicated multi-mile paved trail.
amenities.pickleball: 2 dedicated pickleball courts at McDonald Commons.
amenities.library: Madeira Branch CHPL (7200 Miami Ave); renovated 2020.
amenities.community_center: No full rec center; McDonald Commons Community Room reservable.
amenities.concert_series: Street Dance (August); no formal weekly concert series.
amenities.downtown: Walkable Miami Avenue core anchored by McDonald Commons; Memorial Day march + July 4th parade.

schools.note: 3 buildings; one of only ~8 Ohio districts to earn 5 stars across all five report-card categories

sources.label: Madeira & Indian Hill Joint Fire District

cities.name: Mariemont
cities.signature_amenity: Master-planned 1920s 'garden suburb' on the National Register
cities.distinctive: Mary Emery's master-planned village — Tudor / English-village aesthetic. Tiny but historically singular.

photo.alt: Mariemont Inn (1926) on the village square
photo.credit: Photo by Greg Hume via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY-SA 4.0

civic.library: Mariemont Branch · CHPL — recently completed ~$2M renovation
civic.rec_center_name: Mariemont Racquet Club (village amenity); Boathouse on Little Miami

amenities.parks_pools: 15+ parks (50+ ac): Dogwood, Ann Buntin Becker, Bell Tower, Concourse, Mary Emery, Dale, South 80. Municipal pool at 6000 Mariemont Ave (three pools — main, lap, baby).
amenities.trails: South 80: 75+ acres along Little Miami River with ~7 miles of mixed-surface trails, plus community gardens and a campsite.
amenities.pickleball: Mariemont Racquet Club has tennis; dedicated municipal pickleball not advertised.
amenities.community_center: No traditional community center; Racquet Club + Boathouse serve rec functions.
amenities.public_art: Mary M. Emery Memorial Carillon (49-bell, 1929 Bell Tower) is the signature cultural asset. Dale Park statuary. Mariemont Preservation Foundation curates historic landscape.
amenities.farmers_market: Mariemont Farmers Market — Sundays 9:30am-1pm, May-October, at Elementary School lot.
amenities.concert_series: Sunday-evening summer Bell Tower Carillon concerts at Dogwood Park (Memorial Day → Labor Day).
amenities.holiday_lighting: Luminaria Festival in early December — village-wide luminaries plus Old Town Square celebration.
amenities.tree_program: Tree City USA not confirmed (village known for tree-lined streets per 1920s plan).
amenities.downtown: Mariemont Square — master-planned 1920s Tudor-Revival village center with shops, Mariemont Inn, historic Mariemont Theatre. National Historic Landmark (designated 2007).

schools.note: Small district; pool membership tied to MCSD residency

cities.name: Medina
cities.distinctive: County-seat city. Size match to Hudson, but median HH income roughly half. A 'what wealthy suburbs take for granted' contrast.

photo.alt: Uptown Park gazebo on Medina Public Square
photo.credit: Photo by AngelP8823 via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 4.0

civic.library: Medina Library · Medina County District Library (1907 Carnegie-era Franklin Sylvester gift)
civic.rec_center_features: Aquatics, fitness/wellness, youth and senior programming, hot tub, party rentals

amenities.parks_pools: Memorial Pool (seasonal community pool) + 14 parks across 800 acres of city parkland.
amenities.trails: 16+ miles of biking and walking/multipurpose trails through park system.
amenities.pickleball: Pickleball courts throughout the park system.
amenities.dog_parks: Not specifically listed on Parks page.
amenities.library: Medina Library is flagship of Medina County District Library; historic 1907 building.
amenities.community_center: Medina Recreation Center on Weymouth Rd — full-service rec.
amenities.public_art: Historic Public Square with park, gazebo, Victorian architecture.
amenities.farmers_market: Medina Square Farmers Market (Saturdays 9-1, May-Oct, 70+ vendors) + Farmers Market of Medina.
amenities.concert_series: Free summer concerts on the Public Square.
amenities.holiday_lighting: Candlelight Walk and Ice Festival are signature holiday events on the Square.
amenities.downtown: Historic Public Square — late-19th-century Victorian core; among Ohio's most intact courthouse-square downtowns.

cities.name: Ravenna
cities.distinctive: Explicit downward contrast — county seat at roughly a quarter of Hudson's median income.

photo.credit: Photo by JonRidinger via Wikimedia Commons, CC BY 3.0

utilities.stormwater_fee: Yes — city Stormwater Utility (rate not published)

civic.library: Reed Memorial Library (independent, est. 1921 via Judge Cornelius Reed $25K gift; 85K items; 300K circs/year)
civic.rec_center_features: Fitness machines, indoor track, free weights, cycling studio, group fitness room (notably smaller than peers)

amenities.parks_pools: No municipal pool published; 3 named parks (Havre's Woods, John Tontimonia, Chestnut Hills).
amenities.trails: Trailhead on hike-and-bike trail connecting Ravenna to Cuyahoga Valley National Park.
amenities.library: Independent Reed Memorial Library — historic and active community hub.
amenities.community_center: Ravenna Athletic Center is modest (~7,500 sf) compared to peer rec centers.
amenities.downtown: Walkable historic Main Street as Portage County seat — anchor for the Balloon A-Fair festival each September.

amenity_categories.title: Libraries
amenity_categories.blurb: Library systems are not equal. Some are municipal, some are county branches, some — like Hudson's — are privately endowed institutions with their own historical society.
amenity_categories.icon: <svg viewBox='0 0 24 24' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M12 7v14'/><path d='M3 18a1 1 0 0 1-1-1V4a1 1 0 0 1 1-1h5a4 4 0 0 1 4 4 4 4 0 0 1 4-4h5a1 1 0 0 1 1 1v13a1 1 0 0 1-1 1h-6a3 3 0 0 0-3 3 3 3 0 0 0-3-3z'/></svg>

amenity_categories.title: Community & Rec Centers
amenity_categories.blurb: Does the city own and operate a major recreation facility — pools, gyms, climbing walls — or do residents go elsewhere?
amenity_categories.icon: <svg viewBox='0 0 24 24' fill='none' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'><path d='M3 21h18'/><path d='M5 21V7l8-4 8 4v14'/><path d='M9 9v.01'/><path d='M9 12v.01'/><path d='M9 15v.01'/><path d='M9 18v.01'/></svg>

amenity_categories.title: Parks & Pools
amenity_categories.blurb: Park acreage, named flagships, swim culture (municipal pool vs. swim club).

amenity_categories.title: Trails
amenity_categories.blurb: Paved miles, mountain-bike, bridle. New Albany's 80+ mile system is the benchmark.

amenity_categories.blurb: The current prestige metric for Ohio parks & rec. Dedicated complex or shared courts?

amenity_categories.title: Downtown
amenity_categories.blurb: Walkable mixed-use core. Hudson's First & Main; New Albany Village Center; Worthington's high street.

amenity_categories.blurb: Seasonal vs. year-round (Worthington runs year-round, which is rare).

amenity_categories.title: Concerts & Events
amenity_categories.blurb: Recurring civic programming — summer concert series, outdoor movies, music festivals.

amenity_categories.blurb: How the town shows up for Christmas, July 4th, and the rest. Hudson's downtown lighting is iconic.

amenity_categories.blurb: Dedicated senior facility vs. some senior programming. Worthington's Griswold Center is the high bar.

amenity_categories.blurb: Municipal or quasi-municipal sheets — most peers rely on regional.

amenity_categories.blurb: Quiet but real. Hudson runs a controlled-archery program; Worthington has a formal Deer Management Program.

amenity_categories.blurb: Tree City USA status, dedicated arborist, sidewalk-conflict policy.

hudson_distinctives.title: Hudson Public Power
hudson_distinctives.body: Municipal electric utility — Hudson buys and distributes its own power, rather than paying FirstEnergy. ~10 Ohio cities total run a municipal electric. Translates to faster restoration and (often) lower rates.
hudson_distinctives.peers: Statewide comparators: Westerville, Wadsworth, Cleveland Public Power, and a handful of others run their own electric — but no other city in this benchmark does. All peers are FirstEnergy (NE Ohio), AEP Ohio (Columbus), or Duke Energy (Cincinnati).

hudson_distinctives.title: Velocity Broadband
hudson_distinctives.body: Municipal fiber-to-the-premises. Originally built for businesses, now serving residences with gigabit service that competes directly with the cable incumbent.
hudson_distinctives.peers: Within this benchmark, only Dublin has equivalent: DubLink (100 Gbps muni backbone) plus Fiber to Every Home. Fairlawn ran a similar municipal-fiber project. Most peers rely on the cable incumbent and AT&T/Verizon DSL/fiber.

hudson_distinctives.title: Hudson Library & Historical Society
hudson_distinctives.body: Privately endowed library — meaning the city is not on the hook for the operating budget the way it would be for a typical municipal library. Won the National Medal for Museum and Library Service in 2009 (the federal government's top library award).
hudson_distinctives.peers: In this benchmark, Bexley Public Library, Rocky River Public Library, Worthington Libraries, Upper Arlington Public Library, Mason Public Library, and Reed Memorial Library (Ravenna) are also independent municipal libraries with their own taxing authority. Hudson is unusual in being privately endowed — the city is not on the hook for operating costs the way it would be for a typical municipal library.

hudson_distinctives.title: First & Main
hudson_distinctives.body: A 12-building, mixed-use addition to downtown built in the early 2000s — designed to extend the historic Green rather than replace it. A widely studied example of New Urbanist infill.
hudson_distinctives.peers: Closest analogs: New Albany Village Center / Market Square (planned Georgian) and Dublin Bridge Park (modern mixed-use). Worthington's Old Worthington high street and Mariemont Square (1920s Tudor, National Historic Landmark) are different vintages of the same idea.

hudson_distinctives.title: The Green & Clock Tower
hudson_distinctives.body: A genuine Western Reserve common — the kind of village green most Ohio towns lost in the 20th century. The clock tower in the middle is the literal image of Hudson.
hudson_distinctives.peers: Chagrin Falls Triangle Park, Worthington Village Green, Medina Public Square (Victorian courthouse square), and Mariemont Square are the comparable historic civic spaces.

hudson_distinctives.title: Holiday lighting tradition
hudson_distinctives.body: The downtown lighting display is genuinely a destination — locals and visitors specifically come to walk First & Main and the Green in November and December.
hudson_distinctives.peers: Chagrin Falls goes big on Main Street (plus the New Year's Giant Popcorn Ball Drop). Mariemont's Luminaria Festival is genuinely distinct. Medina's Candlelight Walk and Ice Festival are square-anchored. Most other peers do tasteful but standard municipal decorations.

aspirational.name: Upper Arlington
aspirational.body: The Ohio gold-standard suburb. Bob Crane Community Center (3200 Tremont Rd) is ~165,000 sq ft across 5 floors with an indoor pool, two gyms, walking track, art gallery, e-sports room, and cafe — winner of top OPRA awards. UA also operates three outdoor pools, the Labor Day Arts Festival (since 1966, 15K+ visitors), and an independent UA Public Library established by Ohio HB 494 in 1967.

aspirational.name: Dublin
aspirational.body: Art in Public Places is the most formal municipal public-art program in Ohio: established 1988, 60+ permanent installations, a 2021 Master Plan, funded by 6% hotel/motel tax. Dublin also operates DubLink — a 100 Gbps municipal fiber backbone — and is rolling out Fiber to Every Home (substantially complete 2026). 130+ miles of paved trails. Dublin has municipal fiber, too — Hudson is not alone in this benchmark.

aspirational.name: Mason
aspirational.body: Mason Community Center (150,000+ sq ft) + the attached Municipal Aquatic Center: zero-depth leisure pool with spray geysers and tot-slide, lazy river / whirlpool, a three-story flume slide, and a 25m × 25yd 11-lane competition pool seating ~800. Mason also has 8 dedicated pickleball courts at Heritage Oak Park, Pawmyra Dog Park (three size-segregated areas), and 330+ acres of developed parks. Municipal water/sewer/stormwater/trash.

aspirational.name: Brecksville
aspirational.body: Too small to be a peer on population, but the Jack A. Hruby Natatorium (2021, $10M expansion) is the aspirational rec-aquatics benchmark for NE Ohio: indoor activity pool with two enclosed waterslides, 6-lane competition pool, and an outdoor pool with a rock-climbing wall, zipline, and dump bucket. Bow hunting allowed for deer management. Cleveland Metroparks Brecksville Reservation (3,026 acres) abuts the city.
